rule.desc: Write type-level tests for complex generics and utility types using `Expect`/`Equal` helpers or `tsd`.
rule.rationale: Type-level tests verify that utility types, generics, and overloads produce the expected types, catching regressions that runtime tests cannot detect.

rule.desc: Use typed mocking utilities that preserve interface contracts. Avoid `as any` in test doubles.
rule.rationale: Typed mocks catch interface changes at compile time. Untyped mocks silently diverge from the real implementation.

rule.desc: Structure tests with Arrange/Act/Assert (or Given/When/Then). One logical assertion group per test.
rule.rationale: Clear structure makes tests readable, debuggable, and maintainable. A single focus per test pinpoints failures.

rule.desc: Use descriptive test names that state the behavior and context, not the implementation.
rule.rationale: Good test names serve as documentation. When a test fails, the name should explain what broke without reading the test body.

rule.desc: Mock external systems (HTTP, DB, clock, filesystem). Do not mock value objects, utility functions, or the module under test.
rule.rationale: Over-mocking couples tests to implementation and reduces confidence. Mock at I/O boundaries to keep tests fast and deterministic.

rule.desc: Use `it.each` or `describe.each` for behavior with multiple inputs. Avoid copy-pasted test bodies.
rule.rationale: Parameterized tests reduce duplication and make it easy to add new cases. The table format documents the input/output contract.
